role: Lecturer in Business Analytics and Statistics (Part-Time)

evidence.velocity_vs_rigour: Design and grade four formative assessments; Weekly formative assessments: Each week: academic assessment task in exam conditions, 60–90 minutes
evidence.domain_risk: Lecturer in Business Analytics and Statistics
evidence.collaboration_width: Design and deliver seminars adopting the flipped classroom approach based on London School of Economics material; Design and grade four formative assessments; Teach 22 two-hour weekly seminars
evidence.data_team_maturity: Design and deliver seminars adopting the flipped classroom approach based on London School of Economics material
evidence.jd_authorship: Teach 22 two-hour weekly seminars; Design and grade four formative assessments; Provide approximately 15 hours of individual tutoring
evidence.stakeholder_orientation: Aimed at managers who need to be 'more inquisitive, more precise, more accurate in their statements, more selective in their use of data, more critical of advice given to them.'
evidence.autonomy_level: Design and deliver seminars adopting the flipped classroom approach based on London School of Economics material
evidence.greenfield_vs_fix: Design and deliver seminars adopting the flipped classroom approach based on London School of Economics material
evidence.greenfield_vs_fix_explanation: Delivering seminars using an established external curriculum (LSE material) reflects adapting and running existing course content rather than building new infrastructure from scratch, supporting fix_scale.
evidence.ai_role_explanation: This is an academic teaching contract in statistics and business analytics with no mention of AI tools, AI-assisted coding, or AI-consuming infrastructure anywhere in the JD.
evidence.testing_framing_explanation: 'Formative assessments' refers to educational grading of student learning, not a software testing practice or data quality ownership, so this maps to absent rather than a testing responsibility.
evidence.loss_aversion_framing_explanation: The JD emphasizes teaching precision and analytical accuracy for students, but carries no incident, SLO, compliance, or audit language typical of loss-aversion framing.
evidence.velocity_vs_rigour_explanation: Multiple rigour signals (assessments, grading, exam conditions, feedback) with no velocity language.
evidence.domain_risk_explanation: Educational teaching role with no financial reporting, compliance, or regulatory consequences.
evidence.data_team_maturity_explanation: The program is built on established curriculum and methodology (not greenfield) with multiple pedagogical components, suggesting an established but moderately-scaled teaching infrastructure.
evidence.jd_authorship_explanation: Specific numbers (22 seminars, 2 hours, 4 assessments, 15 hours) and detailed pedagogical methods indicate hiring manager knowledge.
evidence.stakeholder_orientation_explanation: The role serves to develop data literacy and analytical capability in learners who will become data-informed decision makers and consumers of analytics.
evidence.autonomy_level_explanation: While the lecturer designs seminars and assessments, the curriculum content, pedagogical framework, course structure, and volume are all pre-specified, indicating execution within defined constraints rather than strategic direction-setting.
